summary: Graham argues that politicians proposing wealth taxes don't understand the mathematical equivalence between wealth and income taxes. Using the risk-free rate of return (5%), he demonstrates that a 1% wealth tax is equivalent to a 20% income tax, meaning a 'mere 1%' wealth tax would give many US states the highest tax rates in the world.
key_points: The conversion rate between wealth tax and income tax is approximately 20x, derived from dividing by the risk-free rate of return on capital
A 1% wealth tax is mathematically equivalent to a 20% income tax
Politicians who dismiss a 1% wealth tax as modest would never propose adding 20% to income tax rates
A median US state adding a 1% wealth tax would effectively give its residents the highest marginal tax rate in the world at 61.75%
money_quotes: It's clear from the way most politicians talk about the subject that they not only don't know the answer, but don't even realize there's such a question.
Each 1% of wealth tax is equivalent to 20% of income tax.

In the median case, US state politicians talking about adding a 'mere 1%' wealth tax are talking about causing the residents of their state to have the highest taxes in the world. That's not the sort of decision you make lightly.
The answer's not hard to understand, once you realize the question exists.
key_insight: Politicians proposing 'modest' wealth taxes don't grasp that each 1% of wealth tax is equivalent to 20% of income tax, making their proposals far more radical than they realize.
